text: The surgeon begins the dissection on the right cruce, identifying the avascular plane and using a vessel sealer to manipulate the hernia sac anteriorly. The vessel sealer provides a blunt grip, allowing for precise dissection without risk to the esophagus.

The surgeon completes the anterior dissection and proceeds to take the short gastric vessels, connecting the anterior dissection with the posterior dissection. The posterior dissection begins on the left side and meets the right side, creating a space for mesh placement or a tension-free wrap.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to control the dissection and gains length by chop-sticking the tissue. The repair of the crura is performed using Ethibond sutures with pledgets, employing a slipknot technique to control tension. The sutures are manipulated by lifting the needle side and pulling down on the opposite side to convert a square knot into a slip knot.

The surgeon prepares a heart-shaped mesh, placing it over the four-centimeter posterior dissection area. Tissue is laid over the mesh, ensuring the correct orientation with the 'R' marking facing the abdomen. The wrap is pulled through, ensuring it is tension-free, followed by a shoe-shine maneuver.

The surgeon uses a slip knot technique with pledgets to secure the first suture, ensuring the knot does not rip through the tissue. The assistant helps manage tension, while the surgeon alternates hand positions, chopping down from the top to maintain consistency in the knot-tying process.

The surgeon instructs residents to sew the next two sutures using the Slipknot technique, while performing an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD). The stomach is inflated during the EGD, revealing the stack of coins appearance, indicating the recreation of the hill valve.
